narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 75.2% chance that you would not pass, tipping the scales against admission. This prediction drew heavily from your academic record, particularly your placement in the second decile for grades in both your first and third years of school—far below the top deciles, around the sixth, typically seen among students who go on to pass the bar. These early and later performance indicators stood out as the strongest signals pushing the model toward forecasting failure, suggesting challenges in maintaining the high academic standing that correlates strongly with bar success. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, while close to the 3.24 average for bar passers, provided some counterbalance, nudging the prediction slightly in a more favorable direction by showing solid prior achievement. In contrast, your LSAT score of 34 fell short of the 37.27 average for those who passed, adding to the concerns, though it wasn't the top factor here. Your upper-middle family income aligns well with the majority of successful students, and opting for full-time study matches the norm for passers, but these didn't outweigh the academic decile drags. Compared to admitted applicants, who generally rank much higher in school deciles and boast stronger test scores, your profile signaled higher risk to the model. It's understandable to feel disappointed, especially with your dedication evident in choosing full-time enrollment. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to 37 or above, as it could significantly shift future predictions. Strengthening your recent academic deciles through targeted study habits or additional coursework might also help demonstrate upward momentum. Exploring programs with conditional admission or bar prep support could bridge the gap. Ultimately, many students in similar positions improve their profiles and succeed—your next steps can rewrite this story.
